data.title: Walz: DOJ investigation a 'partisan distraction'
data.description:
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz called the Justice Department’s (DOJ) Minnesota investigation a “partisan distraction” on Tuesday. 

Walz’s comments come as The New York Times reported that federal prosecutors have issued subpoenas to him and at least four other state officials as part of their investigation into fraud in Minnesota. 

In a letter addressed to President Trump and posted on the social platform X on Tuesday, the governor vowed “not to be drawn into political theater” and said the DOJ investigation “does not seek justice.”

“Minnesotans are more concerned with safety and peace rather than with baseless legal tactics aimed at intimidating public servants standing shoulder to shoulder with their community,” he wrote. 

The four other state officials to receive subpoenas from DOJ investigators were Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey, St. Paul Mayor Kaohly Her, Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison and Hennepin County Attorney Mary Moriarty, the Times reported. 

It was reported last week that the DOJ intended to launch an investigation into Walz and Frey over allegations that they had obstructed federal law enforcement’s immigration crackdown operations in the state. 

“My focus has always been protecting the people of this state, not protecting myself,” Walz wrote. “Families are scared. Kids are afraid to go to school. Small businesses are hurting. A mother is dead, and the people responsible have yet to be held accountable. 

“That’s where the energy of the federal government should be directed: toward restoring trust, accountability, and real law and order, not political retaliation,” he continued. “Minnesota will not be intimidated into silence and neither will I.”

data.title: Headline: Kara Swisher whines about tech billionaires escaping California tax
data.description:
Mainstream media mouthpiece Kara Swisher, a journalist known for extremely negative and hostile coverage of tech figures like Elon Musk, is really, really mad that the billionaires of Silicon Valley have figured out one neat trick to avoid paying California’s proposed massive wealth tax: they’re simply going to leave the state. 

I, for one, find it amusing that Peter Thiel, Sergey Brin, David Sacks and Larry Page have so easily foiled the Democrats’ plot to confiscate 5 percent of their wealth. But Swisher is furious. Here she was raging at the billionaires, claiming they owe it to the state of California to pay off the state’s considerable debts. 

“We deserve the taxes from you” — that’s essentially what Swisher is saying. My question, who is we?  

I feel differently than her, and I expect most people watching this do as well. Let’s state our values plainly: No one is entitled to the fruits of another person’s labors. No one deserves someone else’s money. No one who earned their fortune legitimately, through their own hard work, should be morally or legally or philosophically obligated to fork it over to the state. 

That doesn’t mean the wealthy should pay zero taxes. Of course the government needs to collect some tax revenue to pay for the upkeep of the things it owns: roads, parks, etc., and to maintain the services that are the rightful purview of the state: courts, police, fire, and so on. We also accept in modern society that we don’t want the needy and the infirm and the elderly to starve or go without medical care, and so we have broadly agreed as a society that the rich will pay more in order to provide for the welfare of the less fortunate, to varying degrees. 

But when a state grossly mismanages its own funds, despite taking in almost $270 billion a year in tax revenue, it is not the responsibility of people who already cough up billions to come to California’s rescue. 

Swisher has the relationship between California and the tech entrepreneurs backward. It is not California that has made the tech sector great. It is the tech sector that has made California great. The state has benefitted from their wealth — from the successful companies they’ve started, from the people they’ve employed, from the technology they’ve created. Yes, it’s a nice place to live, the weather is pretty great. But the weather is nice in Florida, too. And the regulatory policies are even nicer. 

I think that deep down, that’s really what annoys the Kara Swishers of the world: the revelation that under the capitalism system, successful people don’t need the government to help them, they just need it to leave them alone. The European Union is obsessed with strangling its tech sector in the crib, and so they have no major successful tech companies to speak of. Just leave business alone, and you get huge success, and riches, and then sure, you can use some of that money to fund public projects and take care of the poor. But you can’t simultaneously demonize the wealthy and then expect them to bail out a bankrupt state. 

But for the progressive left, it is taken as a fact that “you didn’t build that,” the government did. Remember when President Obama confessed that this was his entire outlook?  

Sorry, Kara Swisher: YOU didn’t build that. Elon Musk did. David Sacks did. Larry Page did. And if you want to confiscate even more of their wealth, then they will build it somewhere else. Somewhere the Republicans are in charge. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Live updates: Trump doubles down on Greenland threats, defends Minnesota ICE presence
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday is doubling down on his threats to seize Greenland, as European allies push back on the president.

This afternoon, he joined White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt for a press briefing to mark one full year of his second term. Earlier Tuesday, Trump released private messages between himself and Emmanuel Macron, in which the French president questioned Trump’s end game with Greenland.

“My friend, we are totally in line on Syria, we can do great things on Iran. I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland,” Macron wrote, per a screenshot shared on Truth Social.

Later on Tuesday, Trump will travel to Davos in Switzerland for the World Economic Forum, where he will meet with several of these allies amid his mounting pressure.

Back in Washington, the Supreme Court released opinions and heard oral arguments on a case involving gun restrictions in Hawaii.

The Senate is in recess, while House members make their way back to Washington as a Jan. 31 shutdown deadline looms. Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.), meanwhile, is in London, where he spoke to the U.K. Parliament. He is the first sitting speaker to do so.

Tensions in Minnesota are still high, as the Trump administration has boosted federal law enforcement’s presence in the state amid massive protests following two Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer-involved shootings in Minneapolis.

Trump, at his Tuesday press conference, said ICE “is going to make mistakes sometimes.”

“They’re dealing with rough people,” he added. 

Follow live below.

data.title: Researchers drop projects, consider switching fields after a year of Trump funding cuts and layoffs
data.description:
After a year of government layoffs and sweeping funding cuts under President Trump, many researchers are hanging on by a thread.

The administration has said it is realigning federal spending to match its agenda, but scientists respond that even proposals that advance the White House’s goals have been ignored or cut. Medical advancements, education research, defense priorities — no area has proven safe from frozen funding, which has also come alongside massive reductions in the government agencies that support these areas.

As Trump marks one year back in office on Tuesday, analysts and professors are considering abandoning their projects or even changing occupations altogether, saying there is no outside group capable of replacing the backing of the U.S. government.

Medical studies in jeopardy

Research published in the journal JAMA Internal Medicine in November found more than 74,000 people had their medical care disrupted last year by cuts to clinical trials by the National Institutes of Health (NIH). From the end of February to August, the Trump administration’s cuts canceled 383 clinical trails, disproportionately affecting research into COVID-19; prevention for cancer, heart disease and brain disease; and behavioral interventions. 

Amy Nunn, professor of public health and medicine at Brown University, was one of many researchers studying HIV prevention or LGBTQ health last year who saw her funding completely canceled. Her study focused particularly on how to keep young, gay Black and Hispanic men in HIV prevention services.  

While she joined a lawsuit that won her the money back in July, she said the repercussions were already too late and delayed the timeline of her study by around a year.  

“We essentially had to stop enrollment in that trial,” Nunn said, noting they had three sites they were working with to enroll individuals. After the funding cuts and other disruptions, Nunn had to cancel subcontracts, these sites lost staff and one of them closed down entirely.  

“We’ve been struggling with enrolling participants because the infrastructure to support the grant has been dismantled,” she added. 

Trump vs. colleges

The billions of dollars in cuts came in both broad slashes across higher education and the targeting of specific universities, with the funding used as leverage by the Trump administration to strongarm institutions into adopting disciplinary policies, admissions and hiring reforms and other measures that align with the president’s view of what higher education should look like.  

It worked a good percent of the time, with schools such as Columbia University cutting deals to get back in favor with the White House.

Harvard University, meanwhile, was one of the few schools to fight back with a lawsuit and get funding restored by the courts.  

Benjamin Singer, vice chair for research at the Department of Medicine at Northwestern University, said he was lucky because his research on how the immune system responds amid severe pneumonia and recovery in the intensive care unit was spared by university funding after the Trump administration pulled $800 million from Northwestern.   

If the university didn’t foot the bill, “the clinical studies that were sponsored by NIH would have needed to stop. Patients would not have seen the potential benefits that come from those studies and the discovery based work also would have had a major shortfall, so those studies would not have been able to continue, either,” Singer said. 

Northwestern eventually struck a deal with the Trump administration to pay a multi-million dollar fine and update its disciplinary policies.  

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Health and Human Services for comment. 

‘Tougher competition for a more limited pool of money’

While many studies got completely canceled, delayed or just barely scraped by, others have seen their proposals ignored altogether. 

A big frustration in the education research landscape has been researchers taking months of planning and time to submit grants, only for them to never be reviewed.  

Robert Kelchen, head of the Department of Education Leadership and Policy Studies at the University of Tennessee in Knoxville, told The Hill the process began as normal when he submitted his grant for research on career and technical education before January 20 of last year. 

“Then the administration came in and just put a halt on all and just canceled review panels. Some of those have been restored, some have not, but you — for the ones that have been restored, that year of funding was largely lost, and that meant that there’s even tougher competition for a more limited pool of money, which means that some high quality research is never going to get funded or done,” Kelchen said. 

Kelchen said he will no longer pursue the research he proposed, lamenting the difficulties his field specifically faces with the severe government layoffs.  

One of the government institutions hardest hit with layoffs was the Institute of Education Sciences (IES), the research arm of the Education Department, which lost 90 percent of its workforce as the administration seeks to close the department entirely. 

Education Week found the Trump administration has disrupted more than $12 billion in K-12 funding that was already allocated by Congress. 

One professor of statistics and data science at Northwestern, whose work has been primarily funded by IES her entire career, told The Hill she might have to switch fields, as two of her grant proposals were ignored and her final one runs out in a year.  

The Hill has reached out to the Education Department for comment.  

Many of the grants that were canceled were given vague reasonings that professors said boiled down to the research not aligning with Trump’s priorities. And while it was not explicitly stated in the formal notifications, researchers say studies that dealt with diversity, equity and inclusion were particularly targeted. 

A five-year grant was recently canceled for Leslie Gonzales, a professor of higher education at the University of Arizona, was recently canceled that was looking to identify practices that led to inclusive mentoring and support of postdoctoral STEM students.  

“All signals point to the fact that this administration since day one has been sort of dead-set to defund and to try in every way possible that they can to criminalize or to scare institutions to believe that it’s criminal to invest in diversity and inclusion types of programming. So, even if it wasn’t expressly stated, it was because of diversity. That’s where all of evidence points,” she said. 

But other say even projects that back Trump’s objectives have not been safe.  

Michael Shields, director of the Center on High-Throughput Materials Discovery for Extremes and professor at Johns Hopkins University, said his organization does work on how to use AI on advanced ceramic materials for defense applications. 

The project, funded by the Army Research Lab at $6 million for the past few years and previously included as a line item in the congressional budget, will be completely dissolved by next year.  

“I do not know the reason why the program is being terminated. I do know that the funds are being reallocated from our program into other into other programs,” Shields said. 

“It’s been a shock for us because our program is directly in line with the stated priorities of the administration. It’s AI and ML [machine learning], it’s materials discovery, it’s defense applications, it’s critical technologies for our defense, for our security and for our competitiveness,” he added. 

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Defense for comment. 

Long-term impacts

The 10 researchers who spoke to The Hill all feared what the future of their fields will look like as the uncertain funding situation could scare off new researchers or cause even established one to avoid taking on hefty projects with the fear the rug could be taken out from under them.  

“It has significant impacts on retention, on recruiting, on training,“ said Heather Pierce, senior director for science policy and regulatory council at the Association of American Medical Colleges. “People who are thinking about going into a career in research, seeing this instability, makes it makes it very difficult to ensure that this remains an attractive career for people … the disruption to funding is problematic for all researchers, but could be disproportionately impact, negatively impactful to early career researchers and trainees.” 

Gonzales said the loss of funding caused one post-doctoral student to stop going to school full time.  

Rebecca Shansky, professor and chair at the Department of Psychology at Northeastern, who runs a behavioral neuroscience lab that studies how memories are formed and how sex differences in the brain are organized, said she has had to lay off most of the individuals in her lab and the lab itself may close in the next six months if she can’t find funding after the Trump administration chose not to approve a grant that received a good score under the Biden administration.  

Even if the funding situation improves, researchers expect what has happened in the past year to have repercussions for decades to come.  

“It’s this snowball, domino effect that’s going to take decades to build back from, if we’re even given the opportunity to,” said Shansky. “This is not about like, ‘Oh, if we just get Democrats in power in the next couple of years, like, everything will just be fine’ … We’re looking at decades of damage that I don’t even know if we’ll see a recovery in my lifetime.”

data.title: Judge posts job listing for prosecutor post Halligan claims to hold
data.description:
A federal judge posted a job opening for interim U.S. attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia on Tuesday, a role that Lindsey Halligan has claimed she has served in legally despite another judge ruling otherwise.

Chief District Judge M. Hannah Lauck entered an order saying that Halligan “may not serve” as interim U.S. attorney for more than 120 days after Attorney General Pam Bondi first appointed her to the post on Sep. 22, 2025. 

Lauck’s order notes that the court may appoint a U.S. attorney until the position is filled by a Senate-confirmed appointee. The court’s vacancy announcement asks interested individuals to submit a cover letter, résumé and confidential questionnaire to Fernando Galindo, the clerk of the court, by no later than Feb. 10. 

Bondi installed Halligan, previously the White House senior associate staff secretary, in September after President Trump forced out Erik Siebert, his initial U.S. attorney nominee, over the latter’s refusal to investigate New York Attorney General Letitia James over alleged mortgage fraud. 

Upon taking office, Halligan launched inquiries into James over the alleged fraud and former FBI Director James Comey over allegations he lied to Congress. 

But U.S. District Judge Cameron Currie ruled in November that Halligan was ineligible to remain in the role and dismissed the cases against James and Comey, arguing the 120-day clock on interim appointments expired during Siebert’s tenure. Currie, an appointee of former President Clinton, stated that the authority to appoint a replacement thereby lied with the district’s federal judges, not Bondi. 

Halligan continued to carry out her duties, however, with U.S. District Judge David Novak ordering her on Jan. 6 to provide an explanation for why she did so despite Currie’s order.

On Jan. 13, Halligan responded to Novak, saying that Currie’s order solely dismissed the cases against Comey and James and does not prevent her from serving as interim U.S. attorney. 

“It is the United States’ position that Ms. Halligan was properly appointed as interim United States Attorney — a position the United States has maintained in part based on internal legal advice from the Department of Justice’s Office of Legal Counsel,” Halligan wrote in an 11-page filing.

“That Judge Currie dismissed two indictments based on her disagreement with that position does not prevent the United States from otherwise maintaining it.”

data.title: Public health groups sue over CDC vaccine recommendations
data.description:
A coalition of medical groups have expanded an ongoing lawsuit against Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to now challenge and reverse the reductions made to childhood vaccine schedule.

A group of plaintiffs including the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP), the American College of Physicians (ACP) and the American Public Health Association (APHA) and others sued HHS under Kennedy in July 2025 to undo updated COVID-19 vaccine guidance that no longer included pregnant women and health children in the recommended demographics.

In its fourth amended complaint, the lawsuit now additionally challenges the decision by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) earlier this month to reduce the number of recommended childhood vaccines from 17 to 11, bringing the country in line with that of Denmark, a nation Kennedy frequently points to when expounding on vaccine guidance.

The plaintiffs called this decision the “most egregious, reckless, and dangerous of the actions Defendants have taken to date” in their amended complaint filed on Monday. They further called for an upcoming meeting of the CDC’s Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to be blocked from being held.

Kennedy fired the entire ACIP last year and remade it with many appointees sympathetic to his views, including many known vaccine critics and skeptics.

The plaintiffs argued the current membership of the committee violates federal law that requires committees to be “fairly balanced” and not be “inappropriately influenced.”

The Hill has reached out to HHS for comment.

data.title: Measles case confirmed at Clemson amid South Carolina outbreak
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A case of measles has been reported at Clemson University as South Carolina grapples with one of the worst outbreaks of the disease in the U.S.

The South Carolina Department of Public Health confirmed “an individual affiliated with the university” has tested positive for the highly contagious disease.

“The individual has isolated, per DPH requirements, and DPH is conducting contact tracing with individuals who may have been exposed and outlining isolation and quarantine protocols,” the agency said.

Health officials didn’t release additional details about the case. Those believed to have been exposed will be notified by email with instructions on quarantining.

According to Clemson Student Health Services, nearly 98 percent of students on the main campus have provided proof of measles immunity.

“The health, safety, and well-being of Clemson’s campus community remains our highest priority,” the university said, noting that guidance was provided to students, faculty, and staff regarding measles preparedness.

The case comes as South Carolina health officials confirm at least 124 measles cases, bringing the state’s total since the outbreak began last fall to 558.

There are at least 171 confirmed measles cases across nine states, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

Measles was declared eliminated in the U.S. in 2000, but cases have resurged, putting the country at risk of losing that status.

The rise in measles cases coincides with efforts by Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to downplay the severity of the disease and the importance of vaccination.

data.title: Recalled tuna accidently shipped to stores in 9 states: How to know if your cans are impacted
data.description:
(WJW) – The U.S. Food and Drug Administration is warning consumers that previously recalled canned tuna was recently shipped to retail stores in several states.

According to a new recall alert shared by the FDA on Monday, a third-party distributor “inadvertently” shipped quarantined canned tuna that Tri-Union Seafoods recalled roughly a year ago.

Officials said the initial recall was issued after the company learned that some of the product’s “easy open” pull tab lids were defective and could cause the cans to leak, “or worse, be contaminated with clostridium botulinum, a potentially fatal form of food poisoning.”

Clostridium botulinum can create a toxin that causes botulism, the U.S. Department of Agriculture explains. The disease can lead to trouble breathing, muscle paralysis, and, in serious cases, death, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Foods that have been improperly canned, preserved, or fermented are known to create the right conditions for the toxin to form.

More recently, a widespread outbreak of infant botulism was linked to recalled baby formula.

The recalled tuna was distributed to specific stores in a handful of states:

  • Meijer: Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and Wisconsin
  • Giant Foods: Maryland and Virginia
  • Safeway, Albertsons, Vons, and Pavilions: California

The cans also bear specific UPC and can codes, as well as best-by dates:

The recall notice from the FDA included the below images of the packaging:

If you have any cans of tuna matching the above descriptions, regulators advise you to not eat it, regardless of whether it looks or smells spoiled. If you have eaten any of the recalled tuna and begin to feel unwell, it’s recommended that you seek immediate medical care.

Cans of tuna impacted by the recall can be returned to the Meijer, Giant Foods, Safeway, Albertsons, Vons, or Pavilions store where they were purchased for a full refund. The FDA notice says customers can also throw the can away or “contact Tri-Union Seafoods directly for a retrieval kit and a coupon for a replacement product.”

data.title: Greene says Trump's campaign endorsements 'solidify the swamp'
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) — who left Congress this month after a bitter spat with the president — said President Trump’s campaign endorsements undermine his purported efforts to “drain the swamp.”

In a post Tuesday on the social platform X, the Georgia Republican urged the public to evaluate potential candidates based on their track record, not just whether they have earned Trump’s endorsement.

“Trump’s endorsements do not drain the swamp, his endorsements solidify the swamp and ensure the swamp is never drained,” she wrote on X.

“Keep voting for Trump endorsed candidates with F voting records and you are responsible for never draining the swamp. Like it or not, that’s the truth,” she added.

Greene pushed back on suggestions that the president’s endorsements are determined by independent staff, saying, “he is not being deceived by his team. It’s not sabotage when he chooses the candidates and controls the agenda.”

Responding to a comment saying Trump’s “endorsement team is awful,” Greene wrote, “Yes the team is uniparty and donor driven, but it ain’t the team, it’s him.”

“He’s bff’s with Lindsey Graham for the love of god,” Greene continued, referring to the longtime South Carolina Republican senator. “No one makes Trump do anything.”

Party leaders, in elections, typically back incumbent candidates, who historically have had the best chance of winning in a primary or general election.

But Greene suggested Trump could “drain the swamp” and pass his preferred policies without interference from Congress if he backed challengers trying to unseat those politicians.

She shared a post that said, “Instead, he endorses against the challengers that are coming to serve as the cavalry and be reinforcements for him.”

Greene, who was long a fierce Trump supporter, said she no longer identifies as part of the MAGA movement after she broke with the president over his handling of the Epstein documents and expiring health care subsidies.

Trump lashed out against her public criticism, calling her a “traitor” and rescinding his endorsement of the congresswoman, who announced her decision to leave Congress shortly thereafter.

data.title: Europe cannot be a 'miserable slave' to Trump: Belgian PM
data.description:
Belgian Prime Minister Bart De Wever said Europe is “under a lot of pressure” from the Trump administration’s tariffs and recent threats to overtake Greenland, while issuing a strong warning to European leaders not to cave to these demands. 

The politician, a member of the conservative Flemish nationalist N-VA party, made the comments during a panel discussion at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, on Tuesday. 

De Wever said Europe is “at a crossroads where it has to decide” to either unite or “explode in any possible direction.” 

“Until now, we tried to appease the new president in the White House,” De Wever said. “We were very lenient, also with the tariffs, we were lenient, hoping to get his support for the Ukraine war. 

“We were in a very bad position at the moment, we were dependent on the United States, so we chose to be lenient, but now so many red lines are being crossed that you have the choice between your self respect,” he continued. “Being a happy vessel is one thing, being a miserable slave is something else. If you back down now, you’re going to lose your dignity, and that’s probably the most precious thing you can have in a democracy is your dignity.”

De Wever said that he and Belgian King Philippe are scheduled to meet President Trump on Wednesday, when the U.S. president joins the conference. 

The prime minister noted that the meeting between the Belgian and American leaders had been arranged prior to “the Greenland issue” and that now, “it will have a different character than we had planned.” De Wever said he plans to tell Trump, “You’re crossing red lines here.”

“We either stand together, or we will stand divided, and if we are divided, there is the end of an era of 80 years of Atlantism really drawing to a close,” he said. 

The Belgian prime minister also paraphrased Italian Marxist philosopher Antonio Gramsci, saying, “If the old is dying and the new is not yet born, then you live in a time of monsters.”

“It’s up to [Trump] to decide if he wants to be a monster, yes or no,” De Wever said.
